seoTitle: Asana Tutorial for Beginners: Task Management Basics
metaDescription: New to Asana? This beginner walkthrough covers creating projects, adding tasks, assigning work, and tracking progress with your team in 2026.
intro: Asana is a project and task management tool designed to help teams organize work, assign responsibility, and track progress in one place. This beginner-friendly guide covers the core steps to get a project up and running.

steps.heading: Create a workspace and project
steps.body: Start by setting up your workspace, then create your first project to hold the tasks you'll be managing.

steps.heading: Add tasks to your project
steps.body: Break your work down into individual tasks and add them to the project, giving each one a clear name and description.

steps.heading: Assign tasks and set due dates
steps.body: Assign each task to the person responsible and set a due date so everyone knows what's expected and by when.

steps.heading: Organize tasks into sections or lists
steps.body: Group related tasks together using sections or a board view, depending on how your team likes to visualize work.

steps.heading: Track progress
steps.body: Use Asana's views, like list, board, or timeline, to monitor how tasks are progressing and spot anything falling behind.

faq.question: Is Asana free to use?
faq.answer: Asana offers a free tier with core task and project management features, with paid plans unlocking more advanced functionality for larger teams.

faq.question: What's the difference between a task and a project in Asana?
faq.answer: A project is a container for related work, while tasks are the individual action items that live inside that project.

faq.question: Can I switch between list and board views?
faq.answer: Yes, Asana lets you view the same project as a list, board, calendar, or timeline depending on what's most useful for the task at hand.

faq.question: How do I know what's assigned to me across all my projects?
faq.answer: Asana's 'My Tasks' view pulls together everything assigned to you across projects, giving you a single place to check your workload.
